title: What is JSON
instruction:
JSON is the universal language of web APIs — every app, every service, every database tool speaks it.

Go makes it simple: `json.Marshal` turns a struct into JSON bytes. Think of it as packing a suitcase — your struct's fields become JSON keys.

Run this and see a Go struct become JSON.
successMessage: A Go struct became JSON. Notice the keys match the field names exactly — capital letters and all. We'll fix that with struct tags next.

title: Struct tags — control the JSON keys
instruction:
By default, JSON keys match Go field names exactly. To use lowercase keys (which APIs expect), add struct tags:

```
type Book struct {
    Title  string `json:"title"`
    Author string `json:"author"`
    Pages  int    `json:"pages"`
}
```

Add `json:"..."` tags to all three fields of `Book` so the output uses lowercase keys.
hint: Title string `json:"title"`  Author string `json:"author"`  Pages int `json:"pages"`

codeChecks.message: Add json:"title" tag to the Title field.
Add json:"author" tag to the Author field.
Add json:"isbn" tag to the ISBN field.
Replace the TODO comments with your struct tags.

successMessage: Lowercase keys — exactly what a REST API expects. Struct tags are how Go bridges the gap between Go naming conventions and JSON naming conventions.

title: omitempty — skip missing fields
instruction:
`omitempty` tells the JSON encoder to skip a field if it is its zero value (empty string, 0, false, nil).

```
Description string `json:"description,omitempty"`
```

Useful for optional fields in library catalogs — only include them when they have a value.

Add `omitempty` to the `Description` and `Edition` tags. Run and see what each book produces.
expectedOutput: {"title":"Clean Code","description":"A handbook of agile software craftsmanship","edition":1}

codeChecks.message: Add omitempty to the Description tag: json:"description,omitempty"
Add omitempty to the Edition tag: json:"edition,omitempty"
Replace the TODO comments with the updated tags.

successMessage: 1984 has no description or edition in the output — omitempty dropped them. Clean catalog responses with no unnecessary nulls.

title: Unmarshal — JSON to struct
instruction:
`json.Unmarshal` parses JSON bytes and fills a Go struct.

```
var b Book
err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(jsonStr), &b)
```

The `&b` is a pointer — Unmarshal needs to write into your variable.

Unmarshal the second JSON string into a `Book` and print its `Title`, `Author`, and `Pages`.
hint: var b Book  err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(jsonStr2), &b)  fmt.Println(b.Title, b.Author, b.Pages)

codeChecks.message: Unmarshal jsonStr2 into a Book struct.
Replace the TODO comment with your unmarshal code.

successMessage: JSON string → Go struct. The tags map "title" → Title, "author" → Author automatically. This is how every Go HTTP handler reads request bodies.

title: Unknown fields are ignored
instruction:
When unmarshaling, Go silently ignores JSON keys that do not match any struct field. This is intentional — it lets you parse partial responses without crashing.

Run this. The JSON has five keys but the struct only has two fields. Go fills what it can and ignores the rest.
successMessage: isbn, pages, and genre were silently ignored. No crash, no error. This is perfect for consuming library catalog APIs — you only map the fields you care about.

title: Nested structs — author details
instruction:
Nested Go structs become nested JSON objects automatically.

Add an `AuthorInfo` struct with `FirstName`, `LastName`, and `BirthYear` fields (all tagged). Nest it inside `Book` as `author`. Marshal the book and see the nested JSON.
hint: type AuthorInfo struct { FirstName string `json:"first_name"`; LastName string `json:"last_name"`; BirthYear int `json:"birth_year"` }  Book has Author AuthorInfo `json:"author"`

codeChecks.message: Define an AuthorInfo struct.
Add FirstName field to AuthorInfo.
Add LastName field to AuthorInfo.
Add BirthYear field to AuthorInfo.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Nested struct → nested JSON object. The author info is cleanly grouped under its own key. Unmarshal works the same way in reverse — Go fills both levels automatically.

title: Slices — multiple authors
instruction:
Go slices become JSON arrays. JSON arrays become Go slices.

`[]string{"Frank Herbert", "Brian Herbert"}` → `["Frank Herbert","Brian Herbert"]`

Run this. Then add a `Tags []string \`json:"tags"\`` field to `Book`, populate it with two genre tags, and see them in the output.

codeChecks.message: Add Tags []string `json:"tags"` field to Book.
Include "fiction" in the Tags slice.
Include "sci-fi" in the Tags slice.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Slices become JSON arrays effortlessly. The same works for []int, []Book, or any slice type — Go handles it all the same way.

title: Maps — dynamic catalog metadata
instruction:
`map[string]T` becomes a JSON object with dynamic keys.

Useful when you do not know the field names ahead of time — like book metadata, translations, or arbitrary key-value stores.

Run this. Then add an entry `"language": "en"` to the metadata map and see it appear in the JSON.

codeChecks.message: Add metadata["language"] = "en" before marshaling.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Maps become JSON objects with their keys as field names — sorted alphabetically by default. Perfect for dynamic data where you cannot define a struct upfront.

title: MarshalIndent — pretty print library inventory
instruction:
`json.MarshalIndent` produces human-readable JSON with line breaks and indentation.

`json.MarshalIndent(v, prefix, indent)`
- prefix — added to the start of each line (usually `""`)
- indent — the indentation string (use `"  "` for two spaces or `"\t"` for tabs)

Switch the marshal call from `json.Marshal` to `json.MarshalIndent` with two-space indentation.

codeChecks.message: Use json.MarshalIndent(lib, "", "  ") for pretty printing.
Replace the TODO comment with your MarshalIndent call.

successMessage: Readable, indented library inventory. Use this for log files, config files, and debugging. Use json.Marshal for API responses where compact output is preferred.

title: Custom marshaling — hide sensitive book metadata
instruction:
Sometimes the default marshaling is not what you want. Implement `MarshalJSON() ([]byte, error)` on your type to take full control.

Common uses: hiding internal IDs, formatting dates differently, computing derived fields.

Run this. `InternalID` is masked in the JSON output by the custom marshaler. Then change the mask from `"***"` to `"[redacted]"`.

codeChecks.message: Change the InternalID mask to "[redacted]".
Replace the TODO comment with your change.

successMessage: The real InternalID never appears in the JSON. Custom marshalers give you complete control — field hiding, computed values, formatting. All without changing the struct.

title: Build a library catalog search response
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a standard library catalog response wrapper:

1. Define a `CatalogResponse` struct with `Status` string (`json:"status"`), `Query` string (`json:"query"`), `Count` int (`json:"count"`), and `Books` interface{} (`json:"books,omitempty"`)
2. Write a `found(query string, books interface{}) string` function that returns a pretty-printed JSON string with `status: "ok"` and the given books and count
3. Write a `notFound(query string) string` function that returns `status: "not_found"` and the query (no books)
4. Call both and print the results

Expected output:
```json
{
  "status": "ok",
  "query": "asimov",
  "count": 1,
  "books": [
    {
      "title": "Foundation",
      "author": "Isaac Asimov"
    }
  ]
}
{
  "status": "not_found",
  "query": "nonexistent"
}
```
hint: func found(query string, books interface{}) string { r := CatalogResponse{Status: "ok", Query: query, Count: ...}; b, _ := json.MarshalIndent(r, "", "  "); return string(b) }

codeChecks.message: Define a CatalogResponse struct.
Add json:"status" tag to the Status field.
Add json:"count" tag to the Count field.
Use omitempty on the Books field.
Define a found() function.
Define a notFound() function.
Use json.MarshalIndent for pretty-printed output.
Set status to "ok" in the found function.
Set status to "not_found" in the notFound function.

successMessage: A reusable library catalog search response wrapper. Every production API uses a pattern like this — consistent shape, clean JSON, omitempty for optional fields. You just built the foundation of a library API.
